metadata.summary: @pytest.mark.parametrize runs one test against many inputs. Covers basic use, descriptive IDs, stacked parametrize for cartesian products, indirect fixture parametrize, and skip/xfail per param.

content.title: pytest Parametrize — Input Matrices, IDs, Stacking, and Indirect
content.summary: @pytest.mark.parametrize eliminates repeated test functions by running a single test body against multiple (input, expected) pairs. Use pytest.param() for per-case marks and ids; stack decorators for cartesian products; use indirect=True to feed params through fixtures.

why.p: Repetitive tests that differ only in their inputs are hard to maintain and easy to forget when adding edge cases. Parametrize declares the full input matrix in one place — adding a new case is one line. Descriptive IDs make failure messages readable: "FAILED test_email[empty_email]" instead of "FAILED test_email[arg0]". Stacked parametrize generates cartesian products automatically, replacing nested loops.

list.item: Validation functions with many valid/invalid input cases.
Business logic with multiple branches that need individual coverage.
Boundary conditions: empty string, None, zero, max value, off-by-one.
Cross-product testing: multiple statuses × multiple roles, or multiple backends.
Converting repetitive similar tests into a single parametrized test.
Tests that require entirely different setup per case — use separate test functions or fixtures instead.
More than ~20 params in one decorator — split into multiple focused test functions for readability.
Cases where the "expected" differs so much that the test body becomes a conditional — that is multiple tests.

section.name: basic-parametrize
section.p: Declare parameters as a comma-separated string of names and a list of value tuples. Each tuple becomes one test case.

rule.statement: Every @pytest.mark.parametrize call that produces more than three test cases MUST supply ids= (or use pytest.param(..., id=...)) so failures print as test_x[descriptive-id] rather than test_x[arg0-arg1].
rule.rationale: Opaque IDs like "arg0" force developers to count positions in the params list to understand which case failed. Descriptive IDs make CI output actionable without opening the source file.

example.title: Email validation with descriptive IDs
example.code: @pytest.mark.parametrize("email,is_valid", [ ("user@example.com", True), ("user.name@example.co.uk", True), ("user+tag@example.com", True), ("invalid", False), ("@example.com", False), ("user@", False), ("", False), (None, False), ]) def test_email_validation(email, is_valid): assert validate_email(email) == is_valid

example.title: Parametrize without IDs — opaque failures
example.note: Add ids=["hello", "world", "empty"] or use pytest.param(..., id="...").

section.name: pytest-param-marks
section.p: pytest.param() wraps a parameter set and lets you attach marks or an id to individual cases without affecting others.

example.title: Parametrize with per-case IDs and marks
Skip and xfail per param case
example.note: marks= accepts a single mark or a list. This is cleaner than a conditional skip inside the test body.

section.name: stacked-parametrize
section.p: Stacking two @pytest.mark.parametrize decorators generates the cartesian product — every combination of the two lists. N x M test cases from N + M declarations.

example.title: Cartesian product: status × role matrix (9 tests)
example.note: Stacking generates 3 × 3 = 9 tests. Use sparingly — large products are slow and the intent gets lost.

section.name: indirect-parametrize
section.p: indirect=True routes param values through a fixture before passing to the test. The fixture receives the param via request.param. Use this when each case needs different expensive setup (e.g., a different user type loaded from the database).

example.title: Indirect: user subscription plan via fixture

list.item: Mutable default in params: the same list or dict object is shared across runs if it is constructed once and mutated in a test. Use a fresh copy or a tuple.
Omitting ids= for more than 3 cases — CI output shows "arg0-arg1" and is unactionable without the source.
Stacking more than two parametrize decorators — the resulting cartesian product is usually too large to maintain.
Using parametrize where separate tests with different fixtures are clearer — forcing a single test body to handle radically different scenarios via conditionals is an antipattern.

checklist.check: Supply ids= or use pytest.param(..., id=...) for every case when the test has more than 3 params.
Include edge cases: empty, None, zero, max, off-by-one, duplicate.
Group related parameters together in the params list.
Use pytest.param(..., marks=pytest.mark.skip(...)) per case instead of a conditional skip inside the body.
Prefer indirect parametrize when each case requires different fixture-level setup.
Avoid mutable objects (lists, dicts) as param values — use immutable types or tuples.
Keep stacked parametrize to at most 2 decorators; beyond that, use a single params list of tuples.
